Subject: A Little Update from the Old Shophouse

Hey mate,

Been a bit of a peculiar week over here — the sort that starts heavy and greets you in the morning like a slap of sea wind. But you know me; rather than dwell on that, I found myself looking at the old bar counter again, tracing the nicks in the wood. Each one’s like a little tale left behind. There’s a dent near the third stool from the left — caused by a sailor knocking down his pint in ’46 when news came that the war was finally over. I’ve probably told you this one before, but it always makes me smile to picture the scene: a storm outside, the flicker of the oil lamps, and the whole room holding its breath before bursting into laughter and song.

Tonight I’ll be hosting another of our storytelling nights. I’ve got a fresh yarn about a Penang harbor ghost who supposedly refuses to board any vessel without a decent pint first. The tourists love it, and I suppose I do too — stories like that have a way of keeping the world lighter than it sometimes feels.

Hope things are smooth sailing on your end. Drop me a line when you can.

Cheers,
Arun

conversations.content: Why do some of the most celebrated meals in history seem to have been served in such hushed, almost reverent surroundings?

Ah, that’s a fine observation, my friend — and one with roots reaching deep into both table manners and the theatre of dining itself. If we leaf back through the pages of culinary history, you’ll find that meals of state, royal banquets, and the grand feasts of the bourgeoisie often came with a deliberate choreography: the clink of fine porcelain, the soft thud of silver cutlery, the murmur of conversation kept to a gentle pitch. This wasn’t just about politeness — it was about *framing the food as art*.

In the courts of France during the 18th century, for instance, service à la française laid dozens of ornate dishes out at once. The atmosphere was kept genteel not merely for dignity’s sake, but so the subtleties of fragrance — roasted quail with truffle jus, say — weren’t lost beneath raucous chatter. In Edo-period Japan, the Kaiseki meal evolved alongside tea ceremony — a cuisine so precise and harmonious that loudness would jar against the pacing, almost like coughing in the middle of a sonata.

There’s also the psychological side: quiet heightens the senses. When surroundings are calm, you’re more attuned to delicate flavours, faint aromas, the gentle texture of a slow-cooked lamb shank giving way. Human minds tend to savour more when there isn’t a battle for auditory attention. Think of it as the culinary equivalent of slow reading by lamplight — each bite its own paragraph.

And there’s prestige in it, too. Across cultures, formality and tranquillity often signalled exclusivity; if you can command an entire room’s pace and tone, you’re subtly telling guests, *what you are about to experience deserves your full attention.*

Of course, not every great meal has been a quiet one — tavern roasts and bustling market feasts have their own glory — but historically, when a chef or host wanted to elevate dining into ceremony, they softened the soundscape so each course could speak in its own voice.

You know, I was chatting with a fellow traveller over a pint the other night, and we got to wondering—before rice became the big star in Southeast Asia, what did folks usually fill their bellies with day to day? Especially in port towns like ours, where traders were always coming and going with their own food habits.

Ah, that’s a fine question — and one with more twists and turns than a back alley in old George Town. You see, rice hasn’t always reigned supreme around here, though it’s been the leading act for quite some centuries now.

If you roll back the clock far enough — say, two millennia — many coastal communities in Southeast Asia, including the Malay world, relied on a mix of tubers, sago, millet, and various local grains before wetland rice farming became entrenched. In places with tidal mangroves or swampy ground, the sago palm’s starchy pith was ground into a filling flour, often cooked into a dense porridge or chewy cakes. Inland, millet and barley had their moments, particularly among communities with trade links to the Indian subcontinent.

Port towns like our own — Penang’s predecessors, if you will — were crossroads of trade long before they were mapped as such. Sailors from the Middle East brought flatbreads; the South Indians introduced dosa and idli; the Chinese traders, before they lent rice noodles to the mix, often carried wheat or mung bean noodles for long voyages. Even tapioca root had its heyday during wartime shortages, becoming a staple in coastal diets when rice was scarce or rationed.

The big shift toward rice dominance in maritime Southeast Asia came gradually, tied to irrigation innovations, political economies that taxed rice, and cultural prestige: in many courts, serving polished white rice was a sign of refinement. But in the bustling docks and back alleys, people ate what was available and filling — bread from the Arab baker, a yam mash from the market, or a hearty stew thickened with sago.

That process caramelises the sugars, gives the beans a deep, almost smoky sweetness, and actually coats the grounds with a thin layer of lipid. When you brew those grounds in the traditional sock filter — a cloth bag in boiling water — more of the oils and heavy-bodied compounds stay in the cup. The result? Now compare that with a modern ‘third-wave’ pour-over or V60. Those often use lightly roasted, high-grade arabica beans to highlight delicate floral or fruity notes, with a very clean, filtered extraction. They produce clarity, sparkle, and nuanced aroma — but at the cost of body. Because paper filters trap many of the oils, you end up with a cleaner, tea-like texture. A wonderful experience, sure, but one that’s more about subtlety in the nose than heft on the tongue.

There’s also the cultural element: Kopi-O has always been a no-nonsense, workman’s drink. It’s brewed strong, shared in kopitiams with chatter bouncing off tiled walls, designed to wake you up and ground you — literally and figuratively. A pour-over, meanwhile, often arrives ceremoniously, brewed slowly for contemplation.

In short, your perception of ‘fullness’ comes from a combination of bean type, roast depth, retained oils, and brewing method — but also the atmosphere and intent behind the cup. Kopi-O plants its flag firmly in the realm of the robust and tangible. A pour-over dances lightly in the world of the ethereal. One’s a dockside storyteller with a booming laugh; the other’s a poet reciting in a quiet room. Both have their charms, but they speak in different tongues.
So is the heavier feel of Kopi-O mostly from the oils that survive the brewing, or do the darker roasts themselves contribute to that mouthfeel too?

Both play important roles, and they actually interact to enhance that sensation.

The oils are a major contributor — they’re hydrophobic compounds that float and emulsify in the cup, adding viscosity and a coating effect on your tongue. Because traditional Kopi-O brewing doesn’t use a fine paper filter, those oils stay in the liquid rather than getting trapped.

But the dark roast itself changes the bean’s cell structure: prolonged, high-heat roasting breaks down cellulose walls and caramelises sugars, producing larger soluble compounds and more melanoidins. These give a richer body and a slight chewiness to the texture. They also deepen flavour perception, making the brew feel denser.

So — oils give you the silky, almost slippery aspect, while the roast-generated compounds provide weight and a satisfying thickness. Together, they’re what make Kopi-O feel like it really sits on your palate rather than washing away immediately.

conversations.content: Why do people in group hikes often fall into quiet stretches after a while, even if they start off chatting away?

That’s a wonderful question, because it’s a little window into both human behavior and the way our senses adjust to shared movement in nature.

When a group sets off on a hike, there’s often a burst of energy — everyone saying their hellos, comparing gear, pointing out the view, maybe even swapping little jokes or stories. That chatter serves as a warm-up, both socially and physiologically: it’s our way of syncing with one another, establishing camaraderie and pace.

But after a while, something shifts. As bodies find their rhythm, breathing deepens and footsteps align, conversation often tapers off. This isn’t awkward silence, though — it’s what psychologists sometimes call a ‘communal lull.’ In group hiking, this has three main causes:

1. **Rhythmic entrainment** – Walking at a steady pace naturally draws people into a shared tempo. Once that rhythm sets in, your brain starts allocating energy towards the physical task, making idle chatter less frequent. Your companions will often subconsciously match their breathing cycles and steps, falling into a wordless unity.

2. **Sensory reorientation** – Lulls often occur when the surrounding environment starts speaking louder than human voices. A far-off waterfall, the crunch of gravel underfoot, the smell of damp earth after rain — these stimuli pull attention outward. In traditional societies that travelled on foot, such silences weren’t considered dead air; they were moments of listening to the land.

3. **Reflective bonding** – Interestingly, silence shared during a physical journey can build trust and closeness. Think of it like watching the tide roll in alongside someone on a quiet pier — words aren’t needed, but you’re still together in the experience.

Culturally, you’ll find this dynamic in many places. In Japan, there’s a practice called *shinrin-yoku*, or forest bathing, where silence is prized to deepen one’s connection to the environment. In parts of the Malay Peninsula, Orang Asli trekking parties sometimes walk for hours with minimal talking, saving words for meaningful exchanges at rest stops. Even sailors on long passages at sea speak less once underway, letting rhythm and shared task turn into a kind of unspoken companionship.

So the next time you notice that lull, you can think of it not as conversation running dry, but as the group slipping into harmony with the landscape — like a folk band letting the music breathe between verses. It’s a sign that you’re not just walking through the terrain… you’re becoming part of it, together.

Yes, often they do — and for a couple of interconnected reasons.

Experienced hikers are more attuned to pacing, breathing, and the physical demands of a trail. Once that rhythm clicks, the shift toward quieter, more meditative walking can happen quite naturally.

They’ve also usually built a comfort with shared silence. Many newcomers to hiking might initially feel pressured to ‘fill’ the air with conversation, because that’s what we do in most social settings. Veteran hikers, on the other hand, tend to understand that silence on a trail is not a social void but part of the experience — so they’re comfortable letting it emerge early.

On top of that, experienced hikers may orient their attention outward more quickly. Because they don’t need to think as much about foot placement, gear adjustments, or how to manage fatigue, they can tune into ambient sounds, scents, and sights almost immediately, which encourages that sensory reorientation you mentioned earlier.

In other words, the ‘communal lull’ doesn’t just arrive faster for them — it often arrives with greater depth. It’s less about conversation fading, and more about an intentional, unspoken agreement to walk within the landscape rather than above it.

A salty soup habit or preference for certain rich, comforting dishes can quietly run parallel with genetic susceptibility.

4. **Polygenic inheritance** – Hypertension doesn’t come from one ‘blood pressure gene’ but from the combined effects of multiple tiny genetic variants. If a cluster of these is present in your mother’s family, they can be passed to you, sometimes with a distinct pattern you might notice more than from your paternal line.

It’s important to remember that inheriting a spice blend isn’t the same as cooking every meal with it.
